title: Round to the Nearest Perfect Square

statement.markdown:
*Round to the Nearest Perfect Square*: Given a positive integer, find the square root of the sum of squares of its digits.

Example cases below.

Note: Input may have any number of digits.
statement.outputFormat: the square root of the sum of squares of its digits as a float
statement.notes: You may assume no digit is zero.

examples.explanation: 12 squared is 144, 3 squared is 9. Sum is 153, and square root of 153 is 12.2. Rounded down to 0.
9 squared is 81. Square root of 81 is 9.

hints.text: Start by calculating the sum of the squares of the digits.

editorial.title: Rounding to the Nearest Perfect Square
editorial.intuition: The problem asks us to find the square root of the sum of squares of the digits of a given positive integer. We can start by calculating the sum of the squares of the digits.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to iterate over all possible square roots and check if they are perfect squares.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use the fact that the square root of the sum of squares of the digits is always an integer.
editorial.optimalSolution: We calculate the sum of squares of the digits, round it to the nearest perfect square, then take its square root.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We need to prove that the optimal solution works for all possible inputs.
editorial.dryRun: If the input is 123, the sum of squares of the digits is 12 squared + 3 squared = 153. The square root of this value is 12.3, which we round down to 0.
commonMistakes: Not checking if the input is a positive integer
Not handling the case where the sum of squares is negative
